content_markdown:
A sound emitting source moves along a straight line with speed $v$ relative to an observer at rest.

The speed of sound relative to the medium is $c$. The observer measures the speed of sound emitted by the source as

diagram_metadata.description: A car moving towards a stationary observer sitting in a chair. An arrow labeled v indicates the car's velocity towards the observer. Musical notes are shown above the car.

vibe_explanation: The speed of a wave (like sound) is determined solely by the properties of the medium through which it travels. While the frequency and wavelength change due to the Doppler effect, the speed of the sound waves relative to the stationary medium (and thus the stationary observer) remains constant at $c$.

content_markdown:
Jeremy is walking alongside a building and is approaching a road junction. A fire engine is sounding its siren and approaching the road along which Jeremy is walking.

Jeremy cannot see the fire engine but he can hear the siren. This is due mainly to

diagram_metadata.description: A top-down view showing Jeremy walking left towards a junction, and a fire engine moving up towards the same junction. A large building is positioned between them, blocking the direct line of sight.

vibe_explanation: While the Doppler effect would change the frequency Jeremy hears, the reason he can hear the siren at all despite the building blocking the direct path is diffraction. Sound waves have long wavelengths relative to the size of the building's corner, allowing them to bend around the obstacle into the 'shadow' region where Jeremy is located.

content_markdown: A source of sound emits waves of wavelength $\lambda$, period $T$ and speed $v$ when at rest. The source moves away from a stationary observer at speed $V$, relative to the observer. The wavelength of the sound waves, as measured by the observer is

diagram_metadata.description: No diagram provided.

vibe_explanation: When a source moves away from a stationary observer, the wavefronts are 'stretched' out. In one period $T$, the source moves a distance $d = V \times T$ away from the previous wavefront. Therefore, the observed wavelength $\lambda'$ is the original wavelength $\lambda$ plus the distance the source moved during that cycle, resulting in $\lambda' = \lambda + VT$.

content_markdown: Which **one** of the following diagrams best represents wavefronts produced by a source of sound of constant frequency as it moves at constant speed towards a stationary observer at O?

diagram_metadata.description: Four diagrams (A, B, C, D) showing circular or elliptical wavefronts emitted by a source moving towards a point O. Diagram A shows circular wavefronts bunched together in the direction of motion. Diagram B shows concentric circular wavefronts. Diagrams C and D show non-circular, elongated wavefronts.

vibe_explanation: When a source moves, each wavefront is emitted from a different position. Since the wavefronts themselves expand at the speed of sound in all directions from the point of emission, they remain circular. However, because the source is moving towards the observer at O, the centers of the newer (smaller) circles are shifted closer to O, causing the wavefronts to bunch up in front of the source and spread out behind it. This results in the pattern shown in diagram A.

content_markdown: The speed of sound in still air is $c$. A source of sound moves away from an observer at speed $v$. What will be the speed of sound as measured by the observer?

diagram_metadata.description: No diagram provided.

vibe_explanation: This is a classic conceptual trap in the Doppler Effect. While the frequency and wavelength of the sound change due to the relative motion of the source, the speed of the wave itself is determined solely by the properties of the medium (still air). Since the observer is stationary relative to the air, they will measure the speed of sound as $c$, regardless of how fast the source is moving.

content_markdown: A point source is moving at a constant speed in a straight-line towards the right and emits sound waves of constant frequency. The speed of the source is less than the speed of sound. Which of the diagrams correctly shows the wavefronts emitted by the source?

diagram_metadata.description: Four diagrams (A, B, C, D) showing circular wavefronts emitted by a source moving to the right. Diagram A shows wavefronts touching at the right edge. Diagram B shows wavefronts bunched on the right but not touching. Diagrams C and D show wavefronts crossing each other or the source leading the wavefronts.

vibe_explanation: When a source moves slower than the speed of sound ($v_s < v$), the wavefronts bunch up in the direction of motion (to the right) because the source 'catches up' slightly to the previously emitted waves. However, since it is slower than sound, the wavefronts never cross each other and the source remains inside the most recently emitted wavefront. Diagram B correctly illustrates this bunching effect without the wavefronts touching (which would imply $v_s = v$) or crossing (which would imply $v_s > v$).

content_markdown:
A source S, moving at constant speed, emits a sound of constant frequency. The source passes by a stationary observer O, as shown below.

Which **one** of the following shows the variation with time $t$ of the frequency $f$ observed at O as the source S approaches and passes by the observer.

diagram_metadata.description: A diagram showing a source S moving along a horizontal dashed line with arrows indicating direction. A stationary observer O is located below the path of the source. Below this are four graphs (A, B, C, D) showing frequency f versus time t.

vibe_explanation: As the source approaches the observer, the observed frequency is higher than the source frequency. As it moves away, the observed frequency is lower. Because the source does not pass directly through the observer (it has a non-zero impact parameter), the transition between the high and low frequency is a smooth curve rather than a step function. Crucially, frequency cannot be negative, so the graph must remain above the t-axis.

content_markdown: A police car, sounding its siren, is travelling at constant speed towards a stationary observer. The sound emitted by the siren is of constant frequency. The frequency of the sound as heard by the observer is higher than that heard by the driver of the police car. The reason for this is that

diagram_metadata.description: No diagram provided in the image.

options.label: A
options.text: the wavefronts received by the observer are closer together than the wavefronts received by the driver.

options.label: B
options.text: the speed of the wavefronts is greater as measured by the observer than by the driver.

options.label: C
options.text: the speed of the wavefronts is less as measured by the observer than by the driver.

options.label: D
options.text: the wavefronts received by the observer are further apart than the wavefronts received by the driver.

vibe_explanation: The Doppler effect for a moving source occurs because the source 'catches up' with the wavefronts it has already emitted in the direction of motion. This results in a shorter wavelength (wavefronts closer together) in front of the source. Since the speed of sound in the medium remains constant for both observers, the shorter wavelength results in a higher observed frequency ($f = v/\lambda$).

content_markdown: Which of the following is a correct description of the Doppler effect?

diagram_metadata.description: No diagram provided.

options.label: A
options.text: Change in frequency of light due to motion of the source of light.

options.label: B
options.text: Change in frequency of light due to relative motion between the source of light and the observer.

options.label: C
options.text: Change in observed frequency of light due to relative motion between the source of light and the observer.

options.label: D
options.text: Change in observed frequency of light due to change in velocity of the source of light.

vibe_explanation: The Doppler effect specifically refers to the change in the *observed* frequency (or wavelength) of a wave for an observer moving relative to its source. Option C is the most precise because it specifies that the frequency change is an 'observed' phenomenon rather than a change in the source's intrinsic frequency, and it correctly identifies 'relative motion' as the cause.

content_markdown:
A stationary source of sound emits waves of wavelength $\lambda$, period $T$ and speed $V$. The source now moves with speed $v$ in a straight line away from a stationary observer.

What are the wavelength and the speed of the wave as detected by the observer?

diagram_metadata.description: A table showing four options for the detected wavelength and speed of a sound wave.

vibe_explanation: In the Doppler effect for sound, the speed of the wave through the medium (air) is determined solely by the properties of the medium, so the detected speed remains $V$. When the source moves away from the observer, each successive wavefront is emitted from a position further away. During one period $T$, the source moves a distance $vT$. Therefore, the distance between wavefronts (the wavelength) increases by this amount, resulting in a detected wavelength of $\lambda + vT$.

content_markdown:
Sound of a constant frequency $f_0$ is being emitted by a sound source. An observer O travels in the direction shown at a speed of $0.1v$ where $v$ is the speed of sound.

Which of the following gives the relationship between $f_0$ and the frequency $f$ of observed sound?

diagram_metadata.description: An observer O moving horizontally to the right with an arrow indicating velocity. Below and to the right of the observer's path is a stationary sound source represented by a black dot.

vibe_explanation: The observer is moving towards the source, but not directly along the line connecting them. The Doppler shift depends on the component of the observer's velocity relative to the source. Since the observer is moving at an angle, the relative approach speed is less than $0.1v$. Therefore, the frequency shift will be positive (higher frequency) but less than the maximum shift of $1.1f_0$ that would occur if they were moving directly toward each other.

content_markdown:
During a journey an observer travels at constant speed towards, and then goes beyond, a stationary emitter of sound.

The frequency of the sound as measured at the emitter is $f$. The frequency according to the observer

diagram_metadata.description: A horizontal line with an arrow pointing right labeled 'Path followed by observer' passes above a black dot labeled 'Stationary emitter of sound'.

vibe_explanation: The Doppler effect depends on the relative velocity component along the line connecting the source and the observer. As the observer moves towards the source, the frequency is higher than $f$. As they move away, it is lower than $f$. Since the observer's path does not pass directly through the source, the angle (and thus the radial velocity component) changes continuously, causing the observed frequency to vary throughout the journey.

content_markdown:
A siren at rest emits a sound of frequency $f_0$. The speed of sound in air is $v$. The siren moves away from an observer at rest relative to the siren in a straight line with constant speed $v_s$.

![diagram]

The observer measures a frequency lower than $f_0$ because the

diagram_metadata.description: A diagram showing an observer standing on the left and a car (siren) moving to the right away from the observer with a velocity vector labeled v_s.

options.label: A
options.text: speed at which the sound moves relative to the observer is $v - v_s$.

options.label: B
options.text: speed at which the sound moves relative to the observer is $v_s - v$.

options.label: C
options.text: wavelength measured by the observer is smaller by a factor $\frac{v_s}{v}$.

options.label: D
options.text: wavelength measured by the observer is greater by a factor $\frac{v_s}{v}$.

vibe_explanation: In the Doppler effect for a moving source, the speed of sound in the medium (relative to the observer) remains constant at $v$. The change in frequency is caused by the source 'stretching' or 'compressing' the wavefronts in space. When the source moves away, each successive crest is emitted from a position further away than the previous one, resulting in an increased wavelength $\lambda' = \lambda + \Delta \lambda$, where $\Delta \lambda = v_s T = \frac{v_s}{f_0}$. This makes the observed wavelength greater.

content_markdown: Which of the following wave phenomena is associated with blood flow measurements?

diagram_metadata.description: No diagram provided.

vibe_explanation: Blood flow measurements often utilize ultrasound. When ultrasound waves reflect off moving red blood cells, the frequency of the reflected wave shifts relative to the incident wave. This frequency shift is known as the Doppler effect and is used to calculate the velocity of the blood flow.

content_markdown:
A radar speed gun is used to measure the speed of a car. The car is moving with speed $v$ away from the gun.

The radar emits microwaves of frequency $f$ and speed $c$. Which of the following is the frequency of the microwaves measured at the gun after reflection by the car?

diagram_metadata.description: A diagram showing a car moving to the left with velocity v, away from a stationary radar gun on the right.

vibe_explanation: This problem involves a double Doppler shift. First, the car acts as a moving observer receiving the signal, and then it acts as a moving source reflecting the signal back. Since the car is moving away, the frequency must decrease. For speeds much less than the speed of light ($v \ll c$), the shift for a reflection is approximately $\Delta f \approx \frac{2v}{c}f$. Because the car is moving away, the observed frequency is $f - \Delta f$.

content_markdown: A point source of sound is moving to the right at constant speed. The source emits sound waves of constant frequency. The speed of the source is less than the speed of sound. Which diagram correctly shows the wavefronts of the sound?

diagram_metadata.description: Four diagrams (A, B, C, D) showing circular wavefronts emitted by a source moving to the right. Diagram A shows wavefronts bunched together on the right. Diagram B shows concentric circles. Diagram C shows wavefronts bunched on the left. Diagram D shows wavefronts touching at a single point on the right.

vibe_explanation: When a source moves, it 'catches up' to the wavefronts it emits in the direction of motion. This causes the wavefronts to bunch together in front of the source (to the right in this case) and spread out behind it. Since the source speed is less than the speed of sound, the source remains inside all the wavefronts it has emitted, but shifted towards the leading edge.

content_markdown: A sample of hydrogen on Earth emits a spectral line that is measured by an Earth observer to have wavelength $500\text{ nm}$. The same spectral line is emitted by a galactic source that is moving away from Earth at speed of $0.1c$. What is the wavelength of the galactic spectral line that will be measured by the Earth observer?

diagram_metadata.description: No diagram provided in the image.

vibe_explanation: Since the source is moving away from the observer, the light undergoes a redshift, meaning the observed wavelength must be longer than the emitted wavelength. Using the Doppler formula for light $\frac{\Delta\lambda}{\lambda} = \frac{v}{c}$, we find $\Delta\lambda = 500\text{ nm} \times 0.1 = 50\text{ nm}$. Adding this shift to the original wavelength gives $500 + 50 = 550\text{ nm}$.

content_markdown:
A stationary source of sound emits sound of frequency $f$. A moving observer measures the sound as having the frequency $f'$. The observer is moving directly away from the source at a speed that is $30\%$ of the speed of sound in air.

Which of the following gives the correct value for $\frac{f'}{f}$?

diagram_metadata.description: No diagram present in the image.

vibe_explanation: For a moving observer and a stationary source, the Doppler effect formula is $f' = f \left( \frac{v \pm v_o}{v} \right)$. Since the observer is moving away, we use the minus sign: $f' = f \left( \frac{v - 0.3v}{v} \right) = f \left( \frac{0.7v}{v} \right) = 0.7f$. Therefore, the ratio $\frac{f'}{f} = 0.7 = \frac{7}{10}$.

content_markdown: Light of wavelength $\lambda_0$ is emitted from a nearby galaxy. The light is received on Earth and the wavelength is measured to be $\lambda$ where $\lambda < \lambda_0$. Which of the following correctly describes the speed and direction of motion of the galaxy?

diagram_metadata.description: A table showing four options (A, B, C, D) with columns for Speed and Direction.

vibe_explanation: Since the observed wavelength $\lambda$ is less than the emitted wavelength $\lambda_0$, the light is blue-shifted, meaning the source is moving towards the observer (Earth). For the Doppler effect of light at non-relativistic speeds, the change in wavelength is given by $\Delta \lambda / \lambda_0 = v/c$. Rearranging for speed $v$ gives $v = \frac{\lambda_0 - \lambda}{\lambda_0}c$.

content_markdown:
The diagram shows a train travelling in a straight line at constant speed $v$, as it approaches the platform of a station.

The whistle of the engine is emitting a sound of constant frequency. Which of the following is **not** true for the sound of the whistle heard by an observer on the platform?

diagram_metadata.description: A train moving to the right with velocity v towards a platform where an observer is standing.

options.label: A
options.text: A sudden change in frequency of the sound as the train passes the observer.

options.label: B
options.text: A sound of constant frequency as the train approaches the observer.

options.label: C
options.text: A sound of increasing frequency as the train approaches the observer and of decreasing frequency after the train has passed the observer.

options.label: D
options.text: A sound of constant frequency after the train has passed the observer.

vibe_explanation: According to the Doppler effect formula for a moving source $f' = f \frac{v}{v \mp v_s}$, if the source speed $v_s$ is constant, the observed frequency $f'$ is constant (higher than the source frequency while approaching, lower while receding). Therefore, the frequency does not 'increase' as it approaches; it stays at a constant elevated value until the moment it passes the observer.

content_markdown: A car horn emits sound of frequency $f$. While the horn is sounding, the car moves in a straight line towards a stationary observer. The speed of the car is $0.10v$ where $v$ is the speed of sound. What is the frequency of the sound of the horn heard by the observer?

diagram_metadata.description: No diagram provided in the image.

vibe_explanation: For a source moving towards a stationary observer, the observed frequency $f'$ is given by $f' = f \left( \frac{v}{v - v_s} \right)$. Here, the source speed $v_s = 0.10v$. Substituting this into the formula gives $f' = f \left( \frac{v}{v - 0.10v} \right) = f \left( \frac{v}{0.90v} \right) = \frac{f}{0.90}$.

content_markdown:
An object emitting a sound of frequency $100\text{ Hz}$ orbits in a horizontal circle at a rate of two revolutions per second.

An observer standing a short distance away from the object is able to hear the sound. Which of the following describes the sound the observer is able to hear?

diagram_metadata.description: A diagram showing an object emitting sound moving in a dashed elliptical path representing a horizontal circle. An observer is standing to the right of the circular path on the same horizontal plane.

options.label: A
options.text: A sound of constant frequency but varying in amplitude

options.label: B
options.text: A sound of constantly varying frequency

options.label: C
options.text: A sound with a frequency of $50\text{ Hz}$

options.label: D
options.text: A sound with a frequency of $200\text{ Hz}$

vibe_explanation: Because the object is moving in a circle, its velocity component relative to the observer is constantly changing. Due to the Doppler effect, as the object moves towards the observer, the frequency increases, and as it moves away, the frequency decreases. Since the circular motion is continuous, the observer hears a frequency that varies periodically over time.

content_markdown: A train moves at constant speed whilst emitting a sound wave of frequency $f_0$. At $t = t_0$ the train passes through a station. Which graph shows the variation with time $t$ of the frequency $f$ of the sound wave as measured by an observer standing on the station platform?

diagram_metadata.description: Four graphs (A, B, C, D) showing frequency f on the y-axis and time t on the x-axis. A shows a rising then falling curve. B shows a horizontal line at f0. C shows a gradual rise and fall. D shows a high constant frequency that drops sharply at t0 to a lower constant frequency.

vibe_explanation: When a source moves at a constant speed towards a stationary observer, the observed frequency is constant and higher than the source frequency. As it passes the observer ($t_0$), the frequency drops. As it moves away at constant speed, the observed frequency is constant and lower than the source frequency. Graph D correctly depicts these discrete steps in frequency.

content_markdown:
A train moving at speed $u$ relative to the ground, sounds a whistle of constant frequency $f$ as it moves towards a vertical cliff face.

The sound from the whistle reaches the cliff face and is reflected back to the train. The speed of sound in stationary air is $c$.

What whistle frequency is observed on the train after the reflection?

diagram_metadata.description: A diagram showing a train on a track moving with velocity u towards a vertical cliff face. An arrow indicates the direction of motion.

vibe_explanation: This is a double Doppler effect problem. First, the cliff acts as a stationary observer receiving sound from a moving source (the train), so the frequency at the cliff is $f' = f \frac{c}{c - u}$. Second, the cliff acts as a stationary source reflecting that frequency $f'$, and the train acts as a moving observer moving towards the source, so the final frequency is $f'' = f' \frac{c + u}{c}$. Substituting $f'$ into the second equation gives $f'' = f \frac{c + u}{c - u}$.

content_markdown: A train travelling in a straight line emits a sound of constant frequency $f$. An observer at rest very close to the path of the train detects a sound of continuously decreasing frequency. The train is

diagram_metadata.description: No diagram provided in the image.

options.label: A
options.text: approaching the observer at constant speed.

options.label: B
options.text: approaching the observer at increasing speed.

options.label: C
options.text: moving away from the observer at constant speed.

options.label: D
options.text: moving away from the observer at increasing speed.

vibe_explanation: The Doppler effect formula for a moving source and stationary observer is $f' = f \left( \frac{v}{v \pm v_s} \right)$. For the observed frequency $f'$ to be continuously decreasing, the denominator must be continuously increasing. If the train is moving away, we use the plus sign ($v + v_s$). As the speed $v_s$ of the train increases, the denominator increases, causing the observed frequency $f'$ to decrease continuously.

content_markdown:
A train is approaching an observer with constant speed

$$\frac{c}{34}$$

where $c$ is the speed of sound in still air. The train emits sound of wavelength $\lambda$. What is the observed speed of the sound and observed wavelength as the train approaches?

diagram_metadata.description: A table with two columns: 'Speed of sound' and 'Wavelength', and four rows labeled A, B, C, and D.

vibe_explanation: The speed of sound in a medium is determined by the properties of the medium itself (like air temperature and density), not the motion of the source, so the observed speed remains $c$. For a source approaching an observer, the wavefronts are 'bunched up', resulting in a shorter observed wavelength. Using the Doppler formula for wavelength $\lambda' = \lambda \frac{v - v_s}{v}$, we get $\lambda' = \lambda \frac{c - c/34}{c} = \lambda \frac{33/34c}{c} = \frac{33\lambda}{34}$.

content_markdown: A train approaches a station and sounds a horn of constant frequency and constant intensity. An observer waiting at the station detects a frequency $f_{\text{obs}}$ and an intensity $I_{\text{obs}}$. What are the changes, if any, in $I_{\text{obs}}$ and $f_{\text{obs}}$ as the train slows down?

diagram_metadata.description: A table showing the possible changes for observed intensity and observed frequency.

vibe_explanation: As the train approaches, it gets closer to the observer, so the intensity $I_{\text{obs}}$ must increase because intensity is inversely proportional to the square of the distance. For the frequency, the Doppler effect formula for a source moving towards an observer is $f_{\text{obs}} = f \frac{v}{v - v_s}$. As the train slows down, the source speed $v_s$ decreases, which makes the denominator $(v - v_s)$ larger, thereby causing the observed frequency $f_{\text{obs}}$ to decrease (though it remains higher than the source frequency until the train stops).

content_markdown: On approaching a stationary observer, a train sounds its horn and decelerates at a constant rate. At time $t$ the train passes by the observer and continues to decelerate at the same rate. Which diagram shows the variation with time of the frequency of the sound measured by the observer?

diagram_metadata.description: Four graphs (A, B, C, D) showing frequency measured by observer vs time. Each graph has a dashed horizontal line representing the horn's frequency and a vertical transition at time t.

options.label: A
options.text: Graph showing frequency increasing while approaching (above horn frequency) and decreasing while receding (below horn frequency).

options.label: B
options.text: Graph showing frequency decreasing while approaching (above horn frequency) and decreasing while receding (below horn frequency).

options.label: C
options.text: Graph showing frequency increasing while approaching (above horn frequency) and increasing while receding (below horn frequency).

options.label: D
options.text: Graph showing frequency decreasing while approaching (above horn frequency) and increasing while receding (below horn frequency).

vibe_explanation: The Doppler effect formula for a moving source is $f' = f \frac{v}{v \mp v_s}$. While approaching, $f' = f \frac{v}{v - v_s}$. As the train decelerates, $v_s$ decreases, making the denominator $(v - v_s)$ larger, so the observed frequency $f'$ decreases towards the source frequency. After passing at time $t$, the train is receding, so $f' = f \frac{v}{v + v_s}$. As it continues to decelerate, $v_s$ decreases, making the denominator $(v + v_s)$ smaller, so the observed frequency $f'$ increases towards the source frequency from below.

content_markdown: A train is moving in a straight line away from a stationary observer when the train horn emits a sound of frequency $f_0$. The speed of the train is $0.10v$ where $v$ is the speed of sound. What is the frequency of the horn as heard by the observer?

diagram_metadata.description: No diagram provided in the image.

vibe_explanation: Since the source is moving away from a stationary observer, we use the Doppler effect formula for a receding source: $f = f_0 \left( \frac{v}{v + v_s} \right)$. Substituting $v_s = 0.10v$, we get $f = f_0 \left( \frac{v}{v + 0.10v} \right) = f_0 \left( \frac{v}{1.1v} \right) = \frac{1}{1.1} f_0$.

content_markdown:
A train is sounding its whistle when approaching a train station. Three statements about the sound received by a stationary observer at the station are:

I. The frequency received is higher than the frequency emitted by the train.
II. The wavelength received is longer than the wavelength emitted by the train.
III. The speed of the sound received is not affected by the motion of the train.

Which combination of statements is correct?

diagram_metadata.description: No diagram provided in the image.

vibe_explanation: When a source approaches a stationary observer, the wavefronts are compressed, leading to a shorter observed wavelength and a higher observed frequency (Statement I is correct, Statement II is incorrect). The speed of sound in the medium (air) depends only on the properties of the medium itself, not the velocity of the source (Statement III is correct).

content_markdown: A sound source emits a note of constant frequency. An observer is travelling in a straight line towards the source at a constant speed. As she approaches the source she will hear a sound that

diagram_metadata.description: No diagram provided in the image.

options.label: A
options.text: gets higher and higher in frequency.

options.label: B
options.text: gets lower and lower in frequency.

options.label: C
options.text: is of constant frequency but of a frequency higher than that of the sound from the source.

options.label: D
options.text: is of constant frequency but of a frequency lower than that of the sound from the source.

vibe_explanation: According to the Doppler effect formula for a moving observer $f' = f \left( \frac{v + v_o}{v} \right)$, if the observer's speed $v_o$ is constant and the source is stationary, the observed frequency $f'$ will be constant and higher than the source frequency $f$. The frequency only changes (gets higher and higher) if the observer is accelerating.

content_markdown: When a train travels towards you sounding its whistle, the pitch of the sound you hear is different from when the train is at rest. This is because

diagram_metadata.description: No diagram provided in the image.

options.label: A
options.text: the sound waves are travelling faster toward you.

options.label: B
options.text: the wavefronts of the sound reaching you are spaced closer together.

options.label: C
options.text: the wavefronts of the sound reaching you are spaced further apart.

options.label: D
options.text: the sound frequency emitted by the whistle changes with the speed of the train.

vibe_explanation: The Doppler effect for a moving source occurs because the source 'catches up' with the wavefronts it has already emitted in the direction of motion. This reduces the distance between successive wavefronts (the wavelength $\lambda$), which results in a higher observed frequency (pitch) since the wave speed in the medium remains constant.

content_markdown: The diagram below represents the wavefronts spreading out from a moving source of sound S. The positions of four observers are also shown. If the frequency of the source is $f$, which observer hears a sound closest in value to this frequency when the source is at the position shown?

diagram_metadata.description: A series of non-concentric circular wavefronts produced by a source S moving to the right. Four observers A, B, C, and D are positioned around the source. A is directly in front of the source's motion, D is directly behind, and B and C are at various angles.

vibe_explanation: The Doppler effect shifts the observed frequency based on the relative velocity between the source and the observer. The frequency shift is greatest along the line of motion (A and D) and smallest when the observer is perpendicular to the direction of motion. In this diagram, the spacing between wavefronts at position B is most similar to the 'average' spacing (undistorted wavelength), indicating the smallest frequency shift.

content_markdown: A source of sound moves directly towards a stationary observer. The frequency of the sound detected by the observer is different from the source frequency because

diagram_metadata.description: No diagram provided in the image.

options.label: A
options.text: the loudness of the sound increases as the source moves towards the observer.

options.label: B
options.text: the apparent wavelength of the sound is longer.

options.label: C
options.text: the speed of sound relative to the observer is increased.

options.label: D
options.text: the apparent wavelength of the sound is shorter.

vibe_explanation: When a source moves towards a stationary observer, each successive wave crest is emitted from a position closer to the observer than the previous crest. This 'bunches up' the wavefronts in front of the source, resulting in a shorter apparent wavelength $\lambda'$. Since the speed of sound $v$ in the medium remains constant, the relationship $f = v/\lambda$ means a shorter wavelength results in a higher observed frequency.

content_markdown:
A source S produces sound waves of frequency $f$ and is moving along a straight line as shown below.

Which observer I, II, III or IV could hear a sound of frequency $f$ when the source is in the position shown?

diagram_metadata.description: A diagram showing a source S moving to the right along a dashed horizontal line. Observer I is behind the source, and observer IV is ahead of the source on the same line. Observers II and III are located below the line of motion.

vibe_explanation: The Doppler effect shift depends on the component of the source's velocity relative to the observer. For observers I and IV, the source is moving directly away or towards them, causing a maximum frequency shift. For observers II and III, there is a component of velocity. However, the question asks who hears the original frequency $f$. This only occurs when the relative velocity component is zero, which happens when the source is at the point of closest approach to an observer (where the line from the source to the observer is perpendicular to the velocity). Looking at the geometry, when the source is at position S, it is directly above observer II, meaning the instantaneous velocity vector is perpendicular to the line S-II, resulting in no Doppler shift at that exact moment.

content_markdown:
A stationary source emits sound of frequency $f_0$. An observer is moving towards the source at constant speed along the path indicated by the dotted line. The observer passes very close to the source at time $T$.

Which **one** of the following graphs best shows the variation with time $t$ of the frequency $f$ heard by the observer?

diagram_metadata.description: A diagram showing an observer moving in a straight line towards and then past a stationary source. Below are four graphs (A, B, C, D) plotting frequency f against time t.

options.label: A
options.text: A graph showing a constant frequency higher than $f_0$ before time $T$, followed by a sharp drop at $T$ to a constant frequency lower than $f_0$.

options.label: B
options.text: A graph showing frequency decreasing linearly towards $f_0$ until time $T$, then increasing linearly away from $f_0$.

options.label: C
options.text: A graph showing frequency increasing linearly towards $f_0$ until time $T$, then decreasing linearly away from $f_0$.

options.label: D
options.text: A graph showing a constant frequency lower than $f_0$ before time $T$, followed by a sharp rise at $T$ to a constant frequency higher than $f_0$.

vibe_explanation: When an observer moves at a constant speed towards a stationary source, the observed frequency is constant and higher than the source frequency ($f = f_0(1 + v/c)$). As soon as the observer passes the source and moves away at the same constant speed, the observed frequency immediately drops to a constant value lower than the source frequency ($f = f_0(1 - v/c)$). This results in a step-down function at time $T$.

content_markdown:
A railway engine, travelling at a constant speed on a straight level track is sounding its whistle. The whistle emits a sound of constant frequency. The engine approaches a station platform and passes an observer O standing on the platform at time $t = T$.

Which of the following sketch graphs best shows the variation with time $t$ of the frequency $f$ of the sound heard by O?

diagram_metadata.description: A diagram showing a railway engine moving towards a platform where an observer O is standing. Below are four graphs (A, B, C, D) plotting frequency f against time t.

vibe_explanation: When a source moves at a constant speed towards a stationary observer, the observed frequency is constant and higher than the source frequency. As it passes the observer at $t=T$ and moves away, the observed frequency immediately drops to a lower constant value. This step-change behavior is characteristic of the Doppler effect for a source moving in a straight line past an observer.

content_markdown: A source of sound approaches a stationary observer. The speed of the emitted sound and its wavelength, measured at the source, are $v$ and $\lambda$ respectively. Which of the following is the wave speed and the wavelength, as measured by the stationary observer?

diagram_metadata.description: A table showing four options (A, B, C, D) with columns for 'Wave speed' and 'Wavelength'.

options.label: A
options.text: Wave speed: larger than $v$; Wavelength: larger than $\lambda$

options.label: B
options.text: Wave speed: equal to $v$; Wavelength: larger than $\lambda$

options.label: C
options.text: Wave speed: equal to $v$; Wavelength: less than $\lambda$

options.label: D
options.text: Wave speed: larger than $v$; Wavelength: less than $\lambda$

vibe_explanation: The speed of sound in a medium is determined by the properties of the medium itself, not the motion of the source, so the observer measures the same wave speed $v$. However, because the source is moving toward the observer, each successive wave crest is emitted from a position closer to the observer than the previous one, effectively 'bunching up' the waves and resulting in a shorter observed wavelength (less than $\lambda$).

content_markdown: A fire engine with its siren sounding approaches and passes a stationary observer. The frequency of the sound emitted by the siren is $f_s$. The frequency of the sound measured by the observer is $f_o$. Which of the following describes the relationship between $f_o$ and $f_s$?

diagram_metadata.description: A table showing the relationship between observed frequency and source frequency for a fire engine approaching and moving away from an observer.
labels_to_preserve: Fire engine moving away from observer

vibe_explanation: The Doppler effect states that when a source moves toward an observer, the wavefronts are compressed, leading to a higher observed frequency ($f_o > f_s$). Conversely, when the source moves away, the wavefronts are stretched out, leading to a lower observed frequency ($f_o < f_s$).

content_markdown: A siren on an ambulance emits sound of frequency $f$. The speed of sound in still air is $v$. What is the frequency of the sound observed when the ambulance travels at speed $\frac{v}{10}$ towards a stationary observer?

diagram_metadata.description: No diagram provided in the image.

vibe_explanation: Since the source is moving towards a stationary observer, we use the Doppler formula $f' = f \left( \frac{v}{v - u_s} \right)$. Substituting $u_s = \frac{v}{10}$, we get $f' = f \left( \frac{v}{v - \frac{v}{10}} \right) = f \left( \frac{v}{\frac{9v}{10}} \right) = \frac{10}{9}f$.

content_markdown:
An ambulance emits a sound of frequency $f$ as it travels along a straight road between stationary observers A and B.

Which of the following shows how the frequency of the sound heard by each observer compares with $f$?

diagram_metadata.description: An ambulance moving to the right towards observer B and away from observer A. An arrow above the ambulance indicates the direction of motion.

options.label: A
options.text: Observer A: greater than $f$; Observer B: greater than $f$

options.label: B
options.text: Observer A: greater than $f$; Observer B: less than $f$

options.label: C
options.text: Observer A: less than $f$; Observer B: greater than $f$

options.label: D
options.text: Observer A: less than $f$; Observer B: less than $f$

vibe_explanation: According to the Doppler effect, when a source of sound moves away from an observer (Observer A), the observed frequency decreases (less than $f$). When the source moves toward an observer (Observer B), the observed frequency increases (greater than $f$).

content_markdown: A source of sound moves away from an observer. The observed frequency of the sound differs from the frequency emitted by the source because the

diagram_metadata.description: No diagram provided in the image.

options.label: A
options.text: observed wavelength of the sound is less than the emitted wavelength.

options.label: B
options.text: observed wavelength of the sound is greater than the emitted wavelength.

options.label: C
options.text: speed of sound relative to the observer has decreased.

options.label: D
options.text: speed of sound relative to the observer has increased.

vibe_explanation: When a source moves away from an observer, each successive wave crest is emitted from a position further away than the previous one. This effectively 'stretches' the distance between crests in the medium, resulting in a longer (greater) observed wavelength and a lower observed frequency. The speed of sound in the medium remains constant relative to the medium/observer (in classical physics contexts like this).

content_markdown: A source emits sound of wavelength $\lambda_0$ and wave speed $v_0$. A stationary observer hears the sound as the source moves away. What are the wavelength of the sound and the wave speed of the sound as measured by the stationary observer?

diagram_metadata.description: A table showing four options (A, B, C, D) with columns for Wavelength and Wave speed.

options.label: A
options.text: Wavelength: less than $\lambda_0$; Wave speed: equal to $v_0$

options.label: B
options.text: Wavelength: greater than $\lambda_0$; Wave speed: equal to $v_0$

options.label: C
options.text: Wavelength: less than $\lambda_0$; Wave speed: less than $v_0$

options.label: D
options.text: Wavelength: greater than $\lambda_0$; Wave speed: less than $v_0$

vibe_explanation: The wave speed of sound is determined solely by the properties of the medium (air), so it remains $v_0$ regardless of the source's motion. However, as the source moves away, each successive wave crest is emitted from a further distance, 'stretching' the observed wavelength, making it greater than $\lambda_0$ (redshift effect).

content_markdown: A stationary sound source emits waves of wavelength $\lambda$ and speed $v$. The source now moves away from a stationary observer. What are the wavelength and speed of the sound as measured by the observer?

diagram_metadata.description: A table showing four options (A, B, C, D) with columns for Wavelength and Speed.

options.label: A
options.text: Wavelength: longer than $\lambda$; Speed: equal to $v$

options.label: B
options.text: Wavelength: longer than $\lambda$; Speed: less than $v$

options.label: C
options.text: Wavelength: shorter than $\lambda$; Speed: equal to $v$

options.label: D
options.text: Wavelength: shorter than $\lambda$; Speed: less than $v$

vibe_explanation: The speed of sound in a medium is determined solely by the properties of the medium itself (like temperature and density), not by the motion of the source. Therefore, the speed remains $v$. However, as the source moves away, each successive wave crest is emitted from a position further from the observer, 'stretching out' the distance between crests. This results in a longer observed wavelength (redshift).

content_markdown: An ambulance siren emits a sound of frequency $1200\text{ Hz}$. The speed of sound in air is $330\text{ m s}^{-1}$. The ambulance moves towards a stationary observer at a constant speed of $40\text{ m s}^{-1}$. What is the frequency heard by the observer?

diagram_metadata.description: No diagram present in the image.

vibe_explanation: For a source moving towards a stationary observer, the observed frequency $f'$ is given by $f' = f \left( \frac{v}{v - v_s} \right)$. Here, the source frequency $f = 1200\text{ Hz}$, the speed of sound $v = 330\text{ m s}^{-1}$, and the source speed $v_s = 40\text{ m s}^{-1}$. Substituting these values gives $f' = 1200 \left( \frac{330}{330 - 40} \right) = \frac{1200 \times 330}{290}\text{ Hz}$.

content_markdown:
An ambulance emitting a sound of frequency $f$ is moving towards a point X at a velocity of $+40\text{ m s}^{-1}$. A car is moving away from X at a velocity of $+20\text{ m s}^{-1}$.

The speed of sound is $v$.

What is the frequency detected in the car?

diagram_metadata.description: A diagram showing an ambulance on the left moving right towards point X at 40 m/s, and a car on the right moving right away from point X at 20 m/s.

vibe_explanation: The general Doppler formula for a moving source and moving observer is $f' = f \left( \frac{v \pm v_o}{v \mp v_s} \right)$. Here, the source (ambulance) is moving towards the observer, which tends to increase the frequency (denominator becomes $v - v_s$). The observer (car) is moving away from the source, which tends to decrease the frequency (numerator becomes $v - v_o$). Substituting the values gives $f' = f \frac{v - 20}{v - 40}$.

content_markdown: A source emits sound of frequency $500\text{ Hz}$. The source approaches a stationary observer at $30\text{ m s}^{-1}$. The speed of sound is $330\text{ m s}^{-1}$. What is the frequency of the sound detected by the observer?

diagram_metadata.description: No diagram provided in the image.

vibe_explanation: Since the source is moving towards a stationary observer, we use the Doppler effect formula for a moving source: $f' = f \left( \frac{v}{v - v_s} \right)$. Plugging in the values: $f' = 500 \left( \frac{330}{330 - 30} \right) = 500 \left( \frac{330}{300} \right) = 500 \times 1.1 = 550\text{ Hz}$.
